h1Title: Alcoholic Fatty Liver Disease: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment
heroOverview: Alcoholic fatty liver disease (AFLD) is the earliest and most reversible stage of alcohol-related liver disease, characterized by fat accumulation (steatosis) in more than 5% of hepatocytes as a direct consequence of alcohol metabolism. It affects approximately 90% of people who drink more than 40 g of alcohol per day — about four standard drinks — making it one of the most prevalent drug-related organ injuries worldwide. The condition is usually asymptomatic or causes only mild right upper quadrant discomfort and fatigue; in most cases it produces no jaundice, ascites, or liver failure. The defining clinical feature is reversibility: complete abstinence from alcohol leads to resolution of steatosis in the majority of patients within two to eight weeks. Without abstinence, a proportion of those with fatty liver progress to alcoholic hepatitis, cirrhosis, and liver failure.

keyStats.prevalence: Affects approximately 90% of heavy drinkers (greater than 40 g/day); AFLD is present in 20-30% of the general population of high-income countries when all alcohol-related liver changes are included (GBD 2019 data)
keyStats.demographics: Both sexes affected; women develop steatohepatitis and cirrhosis at lower alcohol doses and after a shorter exposure than men — a dose-effect 1.5-2× higher sensitivity attributed to differences in first-pass metabolism and body composition
keyStats.avgAge: Clinical presentation typically ages 40-60 years following one to two decades of heavy drinking; younger patients increasingly affected as hazardous drinking patterns start in adolescence
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 2.4 billion people drink alcohol globally (WHO 2018); alcohol accounts for approximately 48% of all cirrhosis deaths worldwide (Manthey et al. Lancet 2019)

definition: Alcoholic fatty liver disease (ICD-10: K70.0), also termed alcoholic steatosis or alcoholic hepatic steatosis, is the earliest histological stage in the spectrum of alcohol-related liver disease (ArLD). It is defined by the accumulation of triglycerides within hepatocytes — predominantly macrovesicular fat droplets displacing the nucleus to the cell periphery — affecting more than 5% of the liver parenchyma, as a direct result of ethanol metabolism. The pathophysiology involves two principal mechanisms: ethanol oxidation by alcohol dehydrogenase and cytochrome P450 2E1 (CYP2E1) shifts the hepatic redox state (elevating the NADH:NAD+ ratio), promoting fatty acid synthesis and inhibiting beta-oxidation; simultaneously, ethanol activates the nuclear transcription factors SREBP-1c and ChREBP that upregulate de novo lipogenesis. The result is triglyceride loading of hepatocytes that exceeds the capacity for very-low-density lipoprotein (VLDL) secretion. K70.0 specifically denotes pure alcoholic steatosis without inflammation or fibrosis; progression to alcoholic hepatitis (K70.1), fibrosis (K70.2), or cirrhosis (K70.3) involves distinct additional pathobiological steps driven by oxidative stress, endotoxin-mediated activation of Kupffer cells, and stellate cell fibrogenesis. The condition is managed by gastroenterologists and hepatologists, with addiction medicine specialists central to the underlying alcohol use disorder.

typesClassification.type: Pure alcoholic steatosis (alcoholic fatty liver, K70.0)
typesClassification.description: Fat accumulation without significant inflammation, hepatocyte ballooning, or fibrosis. Fully reversible with abstinence. Transaminases are normal or only mildly elevated; no clinical liver disease in most cases.

Superimposed inflammatory response with hepatocyte ballooning and often Mallory-Denk bodies (eosinophilic protein aggregates). Ranges from mild (elevated enzymes, no jaundice) to severe (bilirubin above 80 µmol/L with high 28-day mortality).

Collagen deposition beginning in the perivenular and pericellular (chicken-wire) pattern around centrilobular hepatocytes. Precirrhotic but may still regress with sustained abstinence in early stages.

Irreversible architectural distortion with regenerative nodules and bridging fibrosis. Complications of portal hypertension (ascites, variceal bleeding, hepatic encephalopathy) and hepatocellular carcinoma risk emerge. Liver transplantation is the definitive option for decompensated cases.

primarySymptoms: Right upper quadrant fullness or mild aching discomfort from hepatic enlargement, present in approximately 60-70% of symptomatic patients with significant steatosis.
Hepatomegaly — a smooth, mildly tender, enlarged liver extending below the costal margin — detectable on physical examination in the majority of heavy drinkers presenting with fatty liver.
Fatigue and general malaise out of proportion to any identifiable metabolic abnormality, often attributed in part to alcohol's direct toxic effects on mitochondrial function.
Loss of appetite and reduced appetite in the morning, partly from alcohol-induced gastric mucosal irritation and delayed gastric emptying.
Nausea, particularly in the morning, reflecting continuing or recent heavy alcohol consumption rather than liver failure per se.
Mild jaundice (scleral icterus) in a minority of patients when steatosis is accompanied by early hepatitis; frank jaundice signals progression beyond pure steatosis.
Peripheral edema from mild hypoalbuminemia in heavy drinkers with combined nutritional deficiency, not necessarily indicating cirrhosis.
Weight gain paradoxically in some patients from caloric contribution of alcohol (7 kcal/g) and sedentary lifestyle, despite nutritional deficiencies in vitamins and protein.
earlyWarningSigns: Asymptomatic elevation of serum gamma-glutamyl transferase (GGT) — the most sensitive laboratory marker of recent heavy drinking — often discovered on routine blood tests or occupational health screening.
AST:ALT ratio greater than 2:1 in a patient with known or suspected heavy alcohol use, a pattern characteristic of alcoholic (as opposed to nonalcoholic) liver disease.
Mild hepatomegaly discovered incidentally on abdominal ultrasound requested for another reason, with no other explanation in a patient who consumes alcohol.

Macrocytosis (mean corpuscular volume above 100 fL) on complete blood count, reflecting alcohol's direct toxic effect on red cell precursors and folate deficiency — a common laboratory clue to unrecognized heavy drinking.
emergencySigns: Sudden deep jaundice (bilirubin above 80 µmol/L), fever, tender hepatomegaly, and elevated white cell count — presentation of severe alcoholic hepatitis, which carries 28-day mortality up to 40% and requires urgent hepatology assessment.
Haematemesis or melena in a patient with known alcohol-related liver disease — possible variceal or peptic bleeding requiring emergency endoscopy.
Confusion, disorientation, or drowsiness with flapping tremor (asterixis) in a jaundiced patient — signs of hepatic encephalopathy requiring urgent hospital admission.

Abdominal distension with shifting dullness in a patient who did not previously have ascites — new onset ascites signals decompensation from cirrhosis and requires prompt assessment for spontaneous bacterial peritonitis.

causes.description: The primary and necessary cause. The threshold for liver steatosis is approximately 20-40 g of ethanol per day (2-4 standard drinks); 90% of those consuming above 40 g/day develop fatty liver. The relationship is dose-dependent: the higher and longer the alcohol consumption, the greater the degree of steatosis and the risk of progression to inflammation and fibrosis.

causes.cause: Shift in hepatic redox state from ethanol oxidation
causes.description: Oxidation of ethanol by alcohol dehydrogenase produces NADH, raising the NADH:NAD+ ratio. Excess NADH inhibits fatty acid beta-oxidation, gluconeogenesis, and the citric acid cycle, while redirecting acetyl-CoA toward fatty acid synthesis. Triglycerides accumulate in hepatocytes faster than VLDL secretion can export them.

Ethanol and its metabolite acetaldehyde activate transcription factors SREBP-1c (sterol regulatory element-binding protein) and ChREBP, increasing expression of fatty acid synthase (FASN) and other lipogenic enzymes. This compounds the impaired fat oxidation to maximize intracellular triglyceride accumulation.

causes.cause: Impaired VLDL secretion from the liver
causes.description: Alcohol inhibits apolipoprotein B synthesis and phosphatidylcholine production required for VLDL assembly, reducing the liver's ability to export the accumulated triglycerides as VLDL particles into the circulation, further loading the hepatocytes with fat.

Heavy drinkers often have diets deficient in choline, methionine, folate, and antioxidant vitamins, all of which are required for hepatic fat export and oxidative defense. Alcohol also directly impairs intestinal absorption of B12, folate, and thiamine, compounding fatty change and predisposing to additional hepatic injury.

riskFactors.description: The dose-response relationship is clear: 90% of individuals drinking above 40 g/day develop steatosis. The pattern of drinking (binge versus chronic daily) also matters — binge drinking produces acute steatosis even in the context of overall moderate weekly consumption.

Women develop liver disease at lower alcohol doses (as little as 20-30 g/day) than men. Lower gastric alcohol dehydrogenase activity, lower body water proportion, and hormonal factors result in higher blood alcohol levels and greater hepatic ethanol load per gram consumed.

Obesity (BMI above 30) synergizes with alcohol to accelerate hepatic steatosis, steatohepatitis, and fibrosis. Alcohol and excess calories both increase hepatic lipogenesis; the combination leads to fat accumulation greater than the sum of individual contributions.

The PNPLA3 I148M variant — present in approximately 20-25% of European and 50% of Hispanic populations — is associated with a 2-4× higher risk of alcoholic steatohepatitis and cirrhosis at comparable alcohol doses. TM6SF2 E167K confers a similar but smaller risk.

HCV infection and alcohol use have synergistic hepatotoxic effects. Alcohol in an HCV-positive patient accelerates fibrosis progression approximately 3-fold compared with alcohol alone and substantially increases the risk of hepatocellular carcinoma.

Insulin resistance accelerates hepatic fat accumulation from both alcoholic and non-alcoholic mechanisms. Diabetic patients with alcohol use disorder are at higher risk of rapid progression from steatosis to cirrhosis.

Continuous daily heavy drinking over five or more years carries higher fibrosis risk than episodic binge drinking. The transition from steatosis to steatohepatitis and fibrosis occurs in approximately 10-35% of heavy drinkers over five to twenty years.

affectedDemographics: Men aged 40-60 years account for the majority of cases in high-income countries, reflecting both higher rates of heavy drinking and longer exposure duration.
Women develop clinically significant alcohol-related liver disease at lower alcohol doses and with a shorter duration of heavy drinking than men.
Hispanic individuals carry a higher prevalence of PNPLA3 I148M variant and have higher rates of alcohol-related cirrhosis per unit of alcohol consumed compared with non-Hispanic white populations.

Low-income countries in Eastern Europe, West Africa, and parts of Asia show rising rates of alcohol-related liver disease following increasing alcohol consumption and limited healthcare access for early detection.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of alcoholic fatty liver disease requires both evidence of significant alcohol consumption and documentation of hepatic steatosis, with exclusion of other causes. The AUDIT-C or CAGE questionnaire is used at initial assessment to quantify alcohol intake; collateral history from family and a review of previous blood tests for GGT trends are also informative. Laboratory investigations typically show elevated serum GGT (the most sensitive marker), a characteristic AST:ALT ratio above 2:1 (values where AST and ALT are both below 300 IU/L), elevated mean corpuscular volume (MCV), and hypertriglyceridemia. Alkaline phosphatase and bilirubin are usually normal in pure steatosis. Abdominal ultrasound is the first-line imaging modality: hepatic steatosis appears as a diffusely hyperechoic (bright) liver with reduced echogenicity posterior to the parenchyma and loss of the portal vein wall echo. Ultrasound detects steatosis when more than 20-30% of hepatocytes are affected. More sensitive non-invasive tools include the controlled attenuation parameter (CAP) on FibroScan (vibration-controlled transient elastography), which quantifies steatosis grades with a sensitivity above 80% for moderate steatosis. Liver biopsy is the gold standard for grading steatosis and staging fibrosis but is reserved for cases where the diagnosis is uncertain, concurrent disease is suspected (non-alcoholic fatty liver, viral hepatitis), or clinical trial enrollment is considered. The MELD and ABIC scores quantify severity in suspected alcoholic hepatitis. Serological screening for viral hepatitis (HBV, HCV), transferrin saturation for haemochromatosis, and autoimmune markers are performed to exclude competing causes of liver disease.

diagnosticTests.test: Liver blood tests (AST, ALT, GGT, alkaline phosphatase, bilirubin)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the pattern of enzyme elevation; AST:ALT ratio above 2:1 with elevated GGT is characteristic of alcoholic liver disease
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard fasting blood draw. Results within a few hours. In pure alcoholic steatosis, AST and ALT are mildly elevated (usually below 3× upper limit of normal); GGT can be 2-10× elevated. GGT normalizes within four weeks of abstinence.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line imaging for hepatic steatosis; detects moderate to severe fat accumulation as a bright, echogenic liver
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Fasting for four to six hours before the scan. Gel applied to the abdomen, probe pressed gently over the liver. Takes 15-20 minutes. Results same day. Cannot reliably detect steatosis below 20-30% of hepatocytes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-invasive quantification of steatosis grade (CAP score) and liver stiffness (fibrosis stage) in a single outpatient measurement
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient lies flat; a small ultrasound probe is placed on the right lower chest wall. Gentle pressure, no pain, takes 5-10 minutes. Best performed after two hours of fasting. Results available immediately.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Macrocytosis (MCV above 100 fL) reflects alcohol-related erythropoiesis and is a marker of ongoing heavy consumption; low platelets signal portal hypertension and possible cirrhosis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. Results within one to two hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Objective biomarker of heavy drinking (more than 50-60 g/day) for the preceding 1-2 weeks; used when alcohol history is uncertain or in medicolegal contexts
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A fasting blood draw. Results in 24-48 hours. CDT normalizes over two to four weeks of abstinence.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold standard for histological confirmation of steatosis grade, detection of inflammation and ballooning (steatohepatitis), and quantification of fibrosis stage
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed under local anesthesia and ultrasound guidance. A small needle is inserted between the ribs. Requires monitoring for four hours post-procedure. Mild shoulder pain is common for 24 hours. Results in three to five days.

treatmentOverview: The cornerstone of treatment for alcoholic fatty liver disease is complete abstinence from alcohol, which reverses steatosis in most patients within two to eight weeks and prevents progression to alcoholic hepatitis and cirrhosis. This is supported by EASL, AASLD, and NICE guidelines as the single most effective intervention for any stage of alcohol-related liver disease. Management of alcohol use disorder — the underlying driver — requires structured support: brief interventions from primary care physicians, referral to addiction medicine services, and pharmacotherapy for alcohol dependence using naltrexone (blocks opioid reward from alcohol, reducing cravings), acamprosate (reduces glutamatergic withdrawal hyperactivity), or disulfiram (produces unpleasant flushing reaction when alcohol is consumed). Baclofen has evidence for use in patients with established liver disease where naltrexone and acamprosate are relatively contraindicated due to renal or hepatic impairment. Nutritional deficiencies are addressed in all patients: thiamine (vitamin B1) 100-300 mg daily is given for at least three to five days to prevent Wernicke's encephalopathy; B-complex vitamins, folate, and zinc supplementation are standard. Nutritional assessment and dietitian input is particularly important in malnourished patients where enteral or parenteral nutrition is sometimes required. Comorbid conditions — metabolic syndrome, type 2 diabetes, obesity — are managed according to their own guidelines, as each amplifies hepatic fat accumulation. In the absence of cirrhosis or steatohepatitis, no specific pharmacotherapy beyond abstinence support and nutritional rehabilitation is currently recommended in international guidelines. Ongoing clinical trials are evaluating obeticholic acid, PPAR-alpha/delta agonists, and FXR agonists for their potential to accelerate resolution of steatosis.

medicalTreatments.name: Naltrexone (50 mg orally daily or 380 mg IM monthly)
medicalTreatments.description: Opioid antagonist that reduces the rewarding effects of alcohol and decreases craving and relapse risk. Contraindicated in acute hepatitis (ALT above 5× upper limit) due to hepatotoxicity risk; safe in compensated liver disease. First-line pharmacotherapy for alcohol dependence per NICE CG115 and AASLD guidance.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces relapse to heavy drinking by approximately 36% versus placebo; number needed to treat to prevent one relapse is approximately 12 (Cochrane 2010, PMID 20824866)

medicalTreatments.name: Acamprosate (666 mg three times daily)
medicalTreatments.description: GABA-B and NMDA receptor modulator that reduces post-withdrawal craving. Preferred in patients with liver disease when naltrexone is contraindicated; excreted renally with no hepatic metabolism.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Continuous abstinence rate approximately double placebo at six months; NNT to maintain abstinence at one year is approximately 11 (Cochrane meta-analysis)

medicalTreatments.name: Thiamine (vitamin B1) and B-complex supplementation (thiamine 100 mg IV or IM for 3-5 days, then oral)
medicalTreatments.description: Corrects thiamine deficiency from malabsorption and inadequate dietary intake in heavy drinkers; prevents Wernicke's encephalopathy (confusion, ataxia, eye-movement abnormalities). Standard of care for all patients presenting with alcohol-related liver disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Thiamine replacement prevents Wernicke's encephalopathy and reduces the risk of permanent Korsakoff psychosis; no RCT evidence but universal guideline recommendation

medicalTreatments.name: Corticosteroids (prednisolone 40 mg/day for 28 days) — for severe alcoholic hepatitis only
medicalTreatments.description: Used when MELD score above 20 or Maddrey Discriminant Function above 32 indicates severe alcoholic hepatitis. Not indicated for pure steatosis. Lille score at day 7 identifies non-responders who should discontinue.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 28-day survival improved by approximately 15% versus placebo in meta-analyses of severe alcoholic hepatitis; no long-term survival benefit beyond 90 days (STOPAH trial PMID 25877826)

medicalTreatments.name: Baclofen (5 mg three times daily, titrated to 10-20 mg three times daily)
medicalTreatments.description: GABA-B agonist with growing evidence for reducing alcohol craving and maintaining abstinence; safe in cirrhosis when other agents are contraindicated.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Abstinence rate at 12 weeks approximately 71% versus 29% on placebo in cirrhotic patients (Addolorato et al. Lancet 2007, PMID 17920916)

alternativeTreatments.name: Motivational interviewing and cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT)
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured psychological therapies that increase motivation to change drinking behavior and develop coping strategies for relapse triggers. CBT plus pharmacotherapy produces higher abstinence rates than medication alone in randomized trials.

alternativeTreatments.name: Silymarin (milk thistle extract, 140 mg three times daily)
alternativeTreatments.description: Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory plant extract used adjunctively in some European centers. A 2005 Cochrane review of 13 randomized trials found no statistically significant effect on liver-related mortality but possible modest transaminase reduction; widely used but not recommended by EASL or AASLD as standard of care.

whySeeSpecialist: Hepatologist referral is indicated for patients with suspected cirrhosis, hepatitis, or who continue to drink despite liver enzyme elevations. Addiction medicine specialist referral is appropriate for any patient with alcohol dependence who cannot achieve abstinence with brief GP intervention and behavioral support.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a hepatologist with a liver disease program that includes addiction medicine, dietitian services, and social work support. The most effective programs integrate liver disease management and alcohol dependence treatment in a single multidisciplinary team rather than treating them as separate problems.
hospitalCriteria: Hepatology service with FibroScan and liver biopsy capability
Alcohol use disorder treatment program or addiction medicine service
Dietitian with expertise in hepatic and alcohol-related malnutrition
Endoscopy suite for variceal surveillance in patients at risk of cirrhosis
Liver transplant evaluation center for advanced alcohol-related liver disease
keyFacilities: Liver disease and hepatology outpatient clinics
Alcohol use disorder treatment centers with medical supervision
Tertiary liver transplant centers for decompensated cirrhosis
Primary care with brief alcohol intervention and liver function monitoring
preventionStrategies: Consume alcohol within nationally recommended limits: no more than 14 units (approximately 112 g ethanol) per week for both men and women in the UK, or no more than one drink per day for women and two per day for men per NIAAA US guidelines.
Have at least two alcohol-free days per week to allow hepatic recovery from steatosis-inducing alcohol episodes.
Undergo GGT and liver function testing annually in heavy drinkers; early detection of steatosis when the liver is still in a reversible stage is a powerful motivator for abstinence.
Maintain healthy body weight: obesity compounds alcohol-related hepatotoxicity, so combined weight management reduces the cumulative liver disease risk.

Seek alcohol dependence treatment early: the longer and heavier the drinking, the greater the fibrosis risk; early pharmacological and psychological support prevents progression to irreversible cirrhosis.
lifestyleModifications: Complete abstinence from alcohol is the single most effective intervention; fatty liver reverses in 80-90% of patients within two to eight weeks of total cessation.
Maintain adequate nutritional intake with a balanced diet rich in protein, B vitamins, and antioxidants, as malnutrition compounds liver injury in heavy drinkers.

Reduce or eliminate unnecessary hepatotoxic medications including paracetamol (acetaminophen) above 2 g/day and all NSAIDs, which are metabolized by CYP2E1 and can amplify alcohol-related liver injury.

Engage with structured alcohol cessation program — AA, SMART Recovery, or clinician-led CBT — as social and psychological support significantly increases long-term abstinence rates over willpower alone.
Monitor body weight and metabolic parameters: achieving a healthy BMI reduces steatosis from both alcoholic and non-alcoholic mechanisms.

Exercise: aerobic exercise three to five times per week for 30-45 minutes reduces liver fat by approximately 20-30% in obese individuals with steatosis from any cause, independent of alcohol abstinence.

recommended: Protein intake of at least 1.2-1.5 g/kg/day to support hepatic regeneration and reverse alcohol-related malnutrition
B-vitamin-rich foods (whole grains, legumes, green leafy vegetables, fortified cereals) to address alcohol-induced deficiencies in thiamine, folate, and B6
Antioxidant-rich foods (berries, citrus, cruciferous vegetables, nuts) to counter oxidative stress from residual CYP2E1 activity
Lean protein sources (fish, eggs, chicken, legumes) and complex carbohydrates to normalize insulin sensitivity and hepatic metabolism
avoid: All alcohol in any form or quantity
High-fructose corn syrup and sugar-sweetened beverages, which drive hepatic de novo lipogenesis by a mechanism similar to alcohol
Highly processed and saturated-fat-rich foods that amplify hepatic triglyceride accumulation
High-dose paracetamol (above 2 g/day) — toxic threshold is lower in active or recently abstinent drinkers due to CYP2E1 induction

exerciseGuidelines: Aerobic exercise (walking, cycling, swimming) for 150-300 minutes per week at moderate intensity reduces hepatic steatosis and improves insulin sensitivity. Resistance training twice per week improves body composition. Exercise is safe in alcoholic fatty liver disease without cirrhosis; patients with advanced fibrosis or cirrhosis should avoid high-impact activities and consult their hepatologist before starting an exercise program.
dailyManagement: Take prescribed thiamine and B-complex vitamins daily, particularly during the first six to twelve months of abstinence.
Attend outpatient liver review at six to twelve weeks after establishing abstinence; liver enzymes and ultrasound confirm steatosis resolution.
Attend addiction medicine or alcohol counseling sessions as scheduled; relapse rates are high in the first six months and require proactive management.
Keep a weekly symptom diary noting appetite, energy, abdominal discomfort, and any alcohol cravings to share with the clinical team.
Monitor weight weekly; gradual weight gain back to a healthy range is expected after resolution of alcohol-related malnutrition.
Avoid over-the-counter medications (cough syrups, mouthwashes) with alcohol content during abstinence therapy.
prognosis: Alcoholic fatty liver disease has an excellent prognosis with abstinence. In patients who achieve sustained abstinence, hepatic steatosis resolves in 80-90% within two to eight weeks; liver enzymes typically normalize within one to three months, and ultrasound appearances return to normal within six to twelve months. The critical determinant of long-term outcome is sustained alcohol abstinence — those who return to heavy drinking within six months have a substantially increased risk of progression to alcoholic hepatitis and cirrhosis. Among those who continue heavy drinking with established steatosis, approximately 10-20% develop alcoholic hepatitis over five to ten years, and 8-20% progress to cirrhosis within the same period. Once cirrhosis is established, five-year survival without transplantation is approximately 50% in compensated disease, falling to below 20% after decompensation. Hepatocellular carcinoma risk in alcohol-related cirrhosis is approximately 1-2% per year, necessitating six-monthly surveillance ultrasound. Patients who receive a liver transplant for alcohol-related liver disease have five-year survival rates exceeding 70%, comparable to transplantation for other indications.
recoveryTimeline: Steatosis resolves on ultrasound in two to eight weeks of abstinence for most patients. Liver enzyme normalization takes four to twelve weeks. Full recovery without progression to hepatitis or fibrosis is expected in the majority who sustain abstinence. Patients with coexistent mild fibrosis may see improvement over six to twelve months.
complications: Progression to alcoholic hepatitis in 10-20% of patients with continued heavy drinking; severe alcoholic hepatitis carries 28-day mortality of up to 40% despite treatment.
Development of cirrhosis in 8-20% of heavy drinkers with steatosis who do not achieve sustained abstinence over five to twenty years of continued exposure.
Hepatocellular carcinoma arising in alcohol-related cirrhosis at approximately 1-2% per year; surveillance with six-monthly ultrasound and alpha-fetoprotein is recommended.

Wernicke's encephalopathy from thiamine deficiency — a preventable complication of alcohol-related malnutrition presenting with confusion, ataxia, and eye-movement abnormalities requiring emergency thiamine replacement.
Alcoholic cardiomyopathy and cardiac arrhythmias from direct myocardial ethanol toxicity, often concurrent with alcohol-related liver disease in heavy drinkers.
Nutritional deficiencies (thiamine, B12, folate, zinc, vitamin D) causing peripheral neuropathy, anemia, immune dysfunction, and impaired wound healing in patients with longstanding heavy drinking.

supportResources.name: British Liver Trust — Alcohol and the Liver
supportResources.description: Evidence-based patient information on alcoholic liver disease stages, treatment, and alcohol cessation support in the UK.

supportResources.name: NIAAA — Rethinking Drinking
supportResources.description: US National Institute on Alcohol Abuse and Alcoholism interactive tool for assessing alcohol use patterns and guidance on cutting down or stopping.

supportResources.name: Alcoholics Anonymous
supportResources.description: Peer-support fellowship for individuals seeking to stop drinking; worldwide network of meetings including online attendance.

supportResources.name: American Association for the Study of Liver Diseases (AASLD) — Patient Center
supportResources.description: Patient resources on liver disease conditions including alcohol-related liver disease, from the leading US hepatology professional society.

confusedWithConditions.name: Nonalcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: NASH and alcoholic fatty liver produce identical histology — steatosis with hepatocyte ballooning, Mallory-Denk bodies, and lobular inflammation — and cannot be distinguished by liver biopsy alone. NASH occurs in the absence of significant alcohol consumption and is driven by obesity, insulin resistance, and metabolic syndrome. Distinguishing between them requires careful alcohol history, CDT testing, and exclusion of metabolic risk factors.

confusedWithConditions.name: Alcoholic liver disease (unspecified)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: K70.9 refers to unspecified alcohol-related liver disease when the histological stage has not been established. Alcoholic fatty liver (K70.0) specifically denotes pure steatosis without significant inflammation or fibrosis. Clinical staging by liver biopsy or non-invasive tests (FibroScan) differentiates between the stages of the ArLD spectrum.

confusedWithConditions.name: Viral hepatitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Viral hepatitis (HBV, HCV) causes liver enzyme elevation and hepatomegaly that may be clinically indistinguishable from alcoholic liver disease. Serology (HBsAg, anti-HCV) and HCV RNA testing rapidly differentiate viral from alcohol-related causes. Co-infection is possible and clinically important as it dramatically accelerates fibrosis progression.

relatedConditions.name: Alcoholic cirrhosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Cirrhosis is the end-stage complication of alcoholic liver disease; alcoholic fatty liver is the first and most reversible stage on the same disease spectrum.

relatedConditions.name: Alcohol use disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: Alcohol use disorder is the necessary underlying condition driving alcoholic fatty liver; treatment of alcohol dependence is inseparable from treatment of the liver disease.

relatedConditions.name: Nonalcoholic steatohepatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: NASH and alcoholic steatohepatitis share histological features and are managed by the same hepatology teams; distinguishing them directs the treatment strategy.

relatedConditions.name: Hepatitis (viral)
relatedConditions.relevance: Viral hepatitis and alcoholic liver disease can coexist and synergize; HCV in particular markedly accelerates alcoholic fibrosis progression.

relatedConditions.name: Alcohol-induced chronic pancreatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Alcohol-induced pancreatitis and alcoholic liver disease share the same causative exposure and frequently coexist in heavy drinkers; combined hepatic and pancreatic disease worsens nutritional status and management complexity.

faqs.question: What is alcoholic fatty liver disease?
faqs.answer: Alcoholic fatty liver disease is the earliest stage of alcohol-related liver damage, where excess fat builds up in liver cells due to alcohol metabolism. It affects approximately 90% of people who drink more than 40 g of alcohol per day. It is usually asymptomatic and fully reversible if alcohol is stopped, but can progress to hepatitis and cirrhosis with continued heavy drinking.

faqs.question: Is alcoholic fatty liver reversible?
faqs.answer: Yes. Alcoholic fatty liver is the most reversible stage of alcohol-related liver disease. Complete abstinence from alcohol leads to resolution of liver steatosis in 80-90% of patients within two to eight weeks, with normalization of liver enzymes within one to three months. This reversal does not occur if drinking continues.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of alcoholic fatty liver?
faqs.answer: Most people with alcoholic fatty liver have no symptoms. Some experience mild right upper abdominal discomfort, fatigue, and nausea. The liver is enlarged on examination in approximately 60-70% of symptomatic cases. Jaundice, ascites, and confusion are not features of pure fatty liver — they indicate more advanced alcoholic hepatitis or cirrhosis.

faqs.question: How is alcoholic fatty liver diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines an alcohol history with blood tests (elevated GGT, AST:ALT ratio above 2:1, raised MCV) and abdominal ultrasound showing a bright, echogenic liver. FibroScan measures liver fat and stiffness non-invasively. Liver biopsy confirms the diagnosis when the cause is uncertain or fibrosis assessment is needed. Viral hepatitis is excluded by serology.

faqs.question: How much alcohol causes fatty liver?
faqs.answer: Approximately 90% of people drinking more than 40 g of ethanol per day — roughly four standard drinks — develop hepatic steatosis. Women develop fatty liver at lower doses (20-30 g/day) due to higher blood alcohol levels per gram consumed. Even heavy episodic (binge) drinking without high daily average consumption can cause transient acute steatosis.

faqs.question: Can fatty liver from alcohol become cirrhosis?
faqs.answer: Yes, but only in a minority. Approximately 8-20% of heavy drinkers with alcoholic fatty liver progress to cirrhosis over five to twenty years of continued heavy drinking. The risk increases substantially with obesity, female sex, PNPLA3 genetic variant, and concurrent viral hepatitis. Sustained abstinence prevents progression in the vast majority.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for alcoholic fatty liver?
faqs.answer: The treatment is alcohol abstinence — it is more effective than any medication. Nutritional rehabilitation with thiamine, B vitamins, and adequate protein supports recovery. Alcohol dependence is treated with naltrexone, acamprosate, or baclofen, combined with psychological therapy. There is no liver-specific drug licensed for pure alcoholic steatosis beyond abstinence and nutritional support.

faqs.question: What is the AST:ALT ratio in alcoholic liver disease?
faqs.answer: An AST:ALT ratio above 2:1, combined with elevated GGT, is characteristic of alcoholic rather than non-alcoholic liver disease. This pattern occurs because alcohol inhibits pyridoxal phosphate (a cofactor required for ALT synthesis) more than AST. Values above 3:1 are particularly suggestive. Both enzymes are usually below 300 IU/L in pure steatosis.

faqs.question: What is GGT and why is it elevated in alcoholic fatty liver?
faqs.answer: Gamma-glutamyl transferase (GGT) is a liver enzyme elevated by alcohol consumption even before significant liver damage occurs. It is the most sensitive laboratory marker of recent heavy drinking. GGT rises within days of sustained heavy drinking and normalizes within three to four weeks of abstinence, making it useful for monitoring recovery and compliance.

faqs.question: Can you have fatty liver without knowing you drink too much?
faqs.answer: Yes. Many people do not recognize that their drinking exceeds safe levels, or underreport consumption. Alcoholic fatty liver is usually asymptomatic and is often discovered incidentally on ultrasound or blood tests for another reason. Structured alcohol history using validated tools like AUDIT-C can clarify drinking patterns that the patient may not associate with health risk.

faqs.question: How long does it take for fatty liver to reverse after stopping drinking?
faqs.answer: Hepatic steatosis typically resolves on ultrasound within two to eight weeks of complete abstinence. Liver enzymes (GGT, AST, ALT) normalize within four to twelve weeks. Full radiological normalization may take three to six months in patients with more severe initial fat accumulation. Reversal requires complete, not partial, abstinence.

faqs.question: What is the difference between alcoholic fatty liver and NASH?
faqs.answer: Alcoholic fatty liver is caused by alcohol and reverses with abstinence. NASH (nonalcoholic steatohepatitis) occurs in non-drinkers and is driven by obesity, insulin resistance, and metabolic syndrome. Both conditions look identical on liver biopsy. Distinguishing them requires careful alcohol history, biomarker testing (CDT), and exclusion of metabolic risk factors.

faqs.question: Does exercise help alcoholic fatty liver?
faqs.answer: Yes. Aerobic exercise for 150-300 minutes per week reduces hepatic steatosis and improves insulin sensitivity independent of alcohol cessation. Resistance training also improves body composition and metabolic health. Exercise is safe in alcoholic fatty liver without cirrhosis. Combined with abstinence, exercise accelerates steatosis resolution.

faqs.question: Is alcoholic fatty liver painful?
faqs.answer: Most cases are painless. About 60-70% of symptomatic patients report mild right upper quadrant fullness or dull ache from hepatic enlargement. Significant pain or tenderness is more typical of alcoholic hepatitis, which involves liver inflammation. Sharp severe abdominal pain in a patient with alcohol-related liver disease should prompt assessment for complications.

faqs.question: Can women get alcoholic fatty liver at lower alcohol doses than men?
faqs.answer: Yes. Women develop alcoholic fatty liver and progress to hepatitis and cirrhosis at lower alcohol doses than men — as little as 20-30 g per day compared with 40-60 g per day for men. Lower gastric alcohol dehydrogenase activity in women produces higher blood alcohol levels per unit consumed, and hormonal factors further increase hepatic susceptibility.

metaTitle: Alcoholic Fatty Liver: Symptoms & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Alcoholic fatty liver: fat buildup in liver cells from heavy drinking, affecting 90% of heavy drinkers. Symptoms, diagnosis, and how abstinence reverses it.
